data.title: Musk’s X submits remedies relating to its blue check mark, EU spokesperson says
data.description: March 13 (Reuters) - Elon Musk’s social media platform X has submitted remedies in relation to its blue checkmark verification feature following a fine of 120 million euros ($137.63 million), a European Commission spokesperson said on Friday. EU tech regulators fined X in December for breaching online content rules, the first such action under a landmark legislation that drew criticism from the U.S. government. Here are the details: * The Commission will now carefully assess the proposedremedies, European Commission spokesperson Thomas Regnier added,without giving details. * X did not immediately respond to Reuters requests forcomment. * The EU action against X had followed a two-year-longinvestigation under the bloc’s Digital Services Act (DSA), whichrequires online platforms to do more to tackle illegal andharmful content. * The European Commission in July 2024 had charged X withdeceiving users, saying that the blue checkmark does notcorrespond to industry practices and that anyone can pay to geta "verified" status. * Bloomberg News first reported on Thursday that X hasagreed to change its verification mechanism in the EuropeanUnion. * The blue checkmark had previously indicated that anaccount belonged to a public figure whose identity was verified,but Musk changed it to indicate it belonged to a paid subscriberafter acquiring X, formerly known as Twitter, in 2022. ($1 = 0.8719 euros)

data.title: German wholesale prices climb 1.2% in February
data.description: Investing.com - German wholesale prices increased 1.2% in February compared with the same month a year earlier, the federal statistics office reported on Friday.The annual rise in wholesale prices marks the latest inflation data point for Europe’s largest economy as it continues to navigate price pressures across various sectors.The statistics office released the February wholesale price index figures, which track the prices charged by wholesalers to retailers and other businesses before goods reach consumers.Wholesale price movements often serve as an indicator of future consumer price trends, as changes at the wholesale level can eventually be passed on to retail customers.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Glenveagh Properties maintains 2026 guidance with strong order book
data.description: Investing.com - Glenveagh Properties has reaffirmed its fiscal year 2026 guidance, with management expressing continued confidence in the group’s growth trajectory and cash flow generation capabilities.The company maintained its FY26 targets across all divisions. For housebuilding, the company expects 1,600 completions with an average selling price exceeding €375,000 and a gross margin above 21%. The partnerships division is projected to deliver a mid-teen gross margin with average annual gross profit of at least €60 million.Group-wide, Glenveagh anticipates total completions of 2,750 units, €45 million in planned land sales, and earnings per share of 21 cents. The company reported that all units scheduled for delivery in 2026 have commenced construction, with a housebuilding order book of 1,252 units representing 78% of planned deliveries.The company noted that a significant proportion of build costs for the year are already covered, with no changes made to margin guidance. Management acknowledged that earnings will be weighted toward the second half of 2026, reflecting the timing of deliveries from sites acquired in late 2024.Glenveagh reported its land bank requires no further material investment to support annual delivery volumes of 2,750 to 3,600 units through 2030. The company expects meaningful cash generation improvement, particularly in the second half of 2026, driven by increasing delivery volumes and the unwind of €142 million in contract assets.The €25 million share buyback program announced in January is expected to run through approximately May. Management indicated that second half 2026 cash generation will support continued reinvestment and capacity for further capital returns.In the partnerships division, Glenveagh added 100 units to three sites currently in advanced discussions, bringing the total to 500 units. The company disclosed its pipeline for the first time, comprising over 7,000 units with an estimated €3 billion net developable value.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Vincorion sets share price at €17 ahead of Frankfurt listing
data.description: Investing.com - Defense and aviation supplier Vincorion announced Friday that its planned listing on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange would give the company a total market capitalization of €850 million ($978.6 million).The German company set a sale price of €17 a share and said it will offer investors up to €345 million of shares. The offer period is expected to start on Monday before a planned first day of trading on March 20.Vincorion manufactures hybrid energy systems, microgrids, emergency power generators, and stabilization systems for military platforms including tanks and air defense systems such as the U.S.-made Patriot missiles.Key investors, which include Fidelity International and Invesco Asset Management, have committed to buy around €105 million of shares, the company said.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Higher gasoline prices could offset a third of tax-cut stimulus, Wolfe says
data.description:
Investing.com - Rising gasoline prices could significantly erode the consumer stimulus expected from the U.S. administration’s One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB), according to Wolfe Research.
"Low gasoline prices were one of the few pillars supporting the administration’s affordability narrative. That now appears to be unraveling and could potentially offset a material portion of the consumer stimulus from the One Big Beautiful Bill," strategist Stephanie Roth said in a note.
[[pro_promotion |
Dive deeper into energy prices outlook with InvestingPro
]]
Roth compared the impact of higher gasoline prices with the roughly $188 billion in consumer stimulus expected from the tax provisions in the bill. Wolfe’s analysis suggests that while the policy should still provide a net benefit overall, rising fuel costs could dilute its impact on household spending.
“With gasoline prices averaging $3.10 per gallon in 2025, a sustained 20% increase (roughly where prices stand today) would represent about a $65bn annual headwind for consumers,” Roth said.
Under that scenario, she estimates that roughly one-third of the aggregate tax cuts would effectively be offset by higher gasoline expenses. However, the effects would vary significantly across income groups.
The strategist highlights that “the top three income quintiles still see a substantial net benefit, the second-lowest quintile is roughly neutral, and the lowest quintile experiences a clear net loss.”
Lower-income households are particularly vulnerable because they receive limited benefits from the tax cuts while still facing higher fuel costs. Roth noted that “the bottom quintile receives little benefit from the tax cuts but still bears the burden of higher gasoline costs.”
She also modeled more severe energy shock scenarios. If gasoline prices were to rise to $5 per gallon and remain elevated through September, Roth estimates the increase in fuel spending would nearly offset the entire consumer benefit from the tax package. Prices rising to $5.50 over the same period would more than erase the stimulus effect.
"And this is before we incorporate the likely higher food prices as a result of the conflict," Roth added.

data.title: Slowing smartphones, strong gaming: BofA weighs in on consumer electronics
data.description: Investing.com- Bank of America said consumer electronic demand trends diverged sharply across key segments in February, with gaming outperforming and smartphones lagging well below seasonal norms.Smartphone weakness showed few signs of reversing, with U.S. spending in the sector down 2% year-on-year in February. But gaming spending surged 23% in February, BofA said. The outlook for smartphones remained weak, with BofA forecasting that rising memory chip prices were also likely to raise costs and limit margins for manufacturers in the coming months. On the other hand, gaming spending remained solid, with analysts describing the strength as broad-based, driven by sustained consumer engagement rather than seasonal factors alone.The brokerage said Logitech, Soitec, Mycronic, and Nordic Semiconductor together represent the bulk of the roughly 19% consumer exposure across BofA’s European IT hardware coverage.Logitech, according to BofA, was well-exposed to gaming markets and stood to further benefit from strong spending in the United States. Smartphones also showed some pockets of strength, with Apple.com traffic up 4% year-on-year in February, while T-Mobile’s website saw an 8% gain, BofA said.Outside gaming and smartphones, BofA noted some resilience in consumer electronics and hobbies, as the sector struggled to recover from a post-COVID lull.

data.title: US allows countries to buy Russian oil already at sea as Iran war hits supplies
data.description:
Investing.com- The U.S. Treasury on Thursday evening issued a 30-day license allowing countries to buy Russian oil and petroleum goods currently out at sea, in a move aimed at stabilizing global energy markets rattled by the Iran war.
The Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control said in a notice that countries could purchase any Russian oil and petroleum products that were put out to sea before March 12, with the notice valid until April 11. 
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the move aimed at promoting “stability in global energy markets” and to “keep prices low as we address the threat and instability posed by the terrorist Iranian regime.” 
[[pro_promotion |Get more breaking news on oil markets and the Iran crisis by subscribing to InvestingPro]]
Washington had earlier this week loosened some restrictions on Russian oil, especially for major importers such as India, to help offset the impact of the Iran war. 
The U.S. move comes amid heightened hostilities in the U.S.-Israel war with Iran, which saw several tanker attacks and port disruptions in and around the Persian Gulf.
Central to this was the Strait of Hormuz, a key shipping passage for roughly 20% of the world’s oil consumption. Iran was seen attacking international ships around the strait.
Iran’s new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei also said on Thursday that Tehran will keep the strait closed as leverage against the U.S. and Israel.
Oil prices had shot up on the development, with Brent crossing $100/barrel for the second time this week. But prices fell after the U.S. Treasury’s announcement.

data.title: Gold prices rise but head for second weekly loss as Iran war spurs inflation fears
data.description:
Investing.com- Gold prices rose in Asian trade on Friday but were still headed for a second straight week of losses amid growing concerns over energy-fueled inflation from the U.S.-Israel war on Iran. 
Bullion saw some relief on Friday as the dollar and oil stalled their advance, especially after the U.S. announced more waivers of Russian crude to offset supply shocks from Iran.
Spot gold rose 0.6% to $5,109.46/oz by 01:14 ET (05:14 GMT), while gold futures fell 0.3% to $5,111.84/oz. 
[[pro_promotion |Get more key price insights on gold by subscribing to InvestingPro]]
Gold heads for second week of losses, remains rangebound
Spot gold was set to lose about 1.2% this week, its second straight week of losses. 
While the yellow metal did see some safe haven demand due to worsening geopolitical conditions in the Middle East, its gains were largely offset by growing concerns over sticky inflation.
Markets feared that the Iran war will keep oil prices elevated for a prolonged period, driving up inflation across the globe and eliciting a more hawkish stance from major central banks. 
This notion saw markets steadily pricing out expectations for any near-term interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, with the central bank widely expected to leave rates unchanged when it meets next week. 
Gold was left trading squarely in a $5,000-$5,200/oz range since the onset of the Iran war. While the yellow metal was still trading up for the year, it now appeared to be struggling to make headway after plummeting from a near $5,600/oz record high in late-January. 
ANZ analysts said in a note that while gold had lagged amid a storm of near-term headwinds, the yellow metal still remained “a key portfolio diversifier, providing protection against a broad range of macro and geopolitical uncertainties.” 
Other precious metals rose on Friday and were nursing a muted performance for the week. Spot silver rose 0.7% to $84.3275/oz, while spot platinum rose 0.5% to $2,143.21/oz. 
PCE inflation data awaited for more cues 
Markets were now awaiting U.S. PCE price index data for more cues on the world’s largest economy. 
The print is the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, and is likely to factor into interest rate expectations. 
Still, the PCE data is for January, and is unlikely to reflect any energy-driven increases in inflation. 
The PCE data comes just days before a Fed meeting, with the central bank widely expected to leave rates unchanged next week. CME Fedwatch showed markets expect rates to remain unchanged until at least September.

data.title: Oil prices trim early losses as Iran supply fears overshadow Russia measures
data.description:
Investing.com- Oil prices fell in Asian trade on Friday but trimmed a bulk of their initial losses as concerns over supply disruptions stemming from the U.S.-Israel war with Iran remained squarely in play.
Prices had initially fallen as much as 1% after the U.S. said it will allow the purchase of Russian oil already at sea to help offset supply shocks from the Iran conflict. 
But crude trimmed a bulk of the early declines, and was also headed for a second week of strong gains as the Iran war- which is at the heart of oil’s latest rally- showed few signs of abating. 
Brent oil futures for May fell 0.1% to $100.34 a barrel by 02:17 ET (06:17 GMT), while West Texas Intermediate crude futures fell 0.4% to $94.05 a barrel.  
[[pro_promotion |Get more key price insights on oil by subscribing to InvestingPro]]
US allows buying of Russian oil already at sea 
The U.S. Treasury on Thursday evening issued a 30-day waiver allowing countries to purchase Russian oil that was put to sea before March 12.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the move was aimed at helping stabilize energy markets amid supply shocks stemming from the war with Iran.
The U.S. had earlier this week issued some waivers for Russian oil purchases, with world no.3 importer India being allowed to ship crude from Moscow. 
The development came as the Iran conflict showed few signs of cooling, with the U.S. also seen promising to release vast amounts of oil from its strategic petroleum reserve to offset supply shocks.
Reports earlier this week showed the International Energy Agency planning a record, 400 million barrel-plus emergency reserve release to offset the impact of the Iran conflict. 
Oil heads for strong weekly gains with no end to Iran war in sight
Brent and WTI futures were set to add between 7% and 9% this week, extending their sharp gains as the Iran conflict showed few signs of de-escalation. 
Crude prices had surged nearly 30% last week. 
The war entered its fourteenth consecutive day on Friday as attacks by Israel and the U.S. on Iran continued, with Tehran launching waves of missile and drone strikes in retaliation, targeting oil infrastructure in several surrounding Middle Eastern countries. 
Tehran vowed to block the Strait of Hormuz– a key shipping route for oil– as leverage against the U.S. and Israel. 
The Hormuz closure and the oil facility attacks ramped up concerns over long-term oil supply disruptions from the conflict. Hormuz is central to these concerns, given that roughly 20% of the world’s oil consumption passes through the strait. 
"The conflict has now moved beyond a short-lived geopolitical shock and into a phase where supply losses are increasingly structural rather than transient," ANZ analysts wrote in a note.
"Price volatility is likely to remain high, but the skew is increasingly to the upside. Importantly, the longer the disruption persists, the higher the price required to restore market balances." 
Markets are largely cautious over a prolonged surge in oil prices, amid fears that high energy prices will drive up inflation and spur a more hawkish stance from global central banks.

data.title: European stocks edge lower as oil hovers above $100 a barrel
data.description:
Investing.com - European stocks opened lower on Friday, as oil prices remained above $100 a barrel despite moves by the U.S. to allow countries to purchase some sanctioned Russian crude in a bid ease a global supply crunch.
By 04:04 ET (08:04 GMT), the pan-European Stoxx 600 index had fallen by 0.7%, the Dax in Germany had slipped by 0.9%, the CAC 40 in France had slid 1.0%, and the FTSE 100 in the U.K. had dropped 0.8%.
Equities in the region received a weak handover from Asia, reflecting little belief among investors that the joint U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran will be over soon. Major indices in South Korea and Japan - both major importers of oil flows coming from the Middle East - fell by more than 1.4%.
Like many Asian nations, several countries in Europe heavily import energy products that pass through the Strait of Hormuz, a vital waterway surrounded on three sides by Iran.
New Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei said on Thursday that the strait will remain closed until the fighting ceases. Container shipping traffic through the choke point has virtually stopped, with companies worried about potential attacks that could endanger the safety of crews. Shipping firms have also been struggling to find insurance for sailings that appear to be increasingly perilous.
Despite efforts in recent days by the U.S. and the International Energy Agency to make more oil available for purchase, the trickle of supply has sent Brent crude surging back above $100 a barrel. Volatility in Brent has been intense. Earlier this week, the global benchmark climbed to as high as nearly $120 a barrel, before temporarily retreating below $90 a barrel.
Still, oil prices remain well above where they were prior to the start of the conflict, fueling concerns over a spike in inflationary pressures around the world that could dent bets for central bank monetary policy easing. In Europe, these fears have pushed up government bond yields in Germany and France, weighing on stocks.
"European and Asian equity markets have been hit harder than those of the US, and the longer the crisis goes on, the greater this divergence will become," analysts at ING said in a note.
Inflation readings in focus
Against this backdrop, traders are pouring through fresh inflation data from France and Spain.
Consumer prices in France, Europe’s second-largest economy, climbed by 1.1% in the twelve months to February on an EU-harmonized basis, in line with estimates and faster than 0.4% in January. A similar measure in Spain accelerated marginally to 2.5%.
Later on Friday, the U.S. personal consumption expenditures price index for January will be released. The metric is closely watched by markets as it is one of the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation readings.
Crucially, however, the numbers cover a period that largely does not include the Iran war, which began with a barrage of U.S. and Israeli air strikes on Iran in late February. The outlook for inflation has darkened since the outbreak of the fighting, especially in Europe, where until recently economists had suggested that price growth had been widely quelled.
